content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

January 4, 2019

Dorine Martirosian
Senior Assistant City Attorney
613 E. Broadway, Suite 220
Glendale, CA 91206

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-18-262

Dear Ms. Martirosian:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Glendale Water & Power Department ("GWP") Commissioner Andy Carrasco regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act") and Section 1090.¹ Please note that we are not providing advice under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest.

Also, note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice.

QUESTIONS

Do Section 1090 and the Act prohibit Commissioner Carrasco, a Southern California Gas Company employee, from taking part in the report and recommendation process relating to the city-owned Grayson Power Plant's Repowering Project ("Project")?

CONCLUSION

Yes. In general, Section 1090 prohibits Commissioner Carrasco from participating in the making of contracts for the Project, because such contracts will implicate his financial interest in the Gas Company. Prohibited conduct includes taking part in the reports presented to the GWP Commission, as well as voting on recommendations to be made to the City Council.

_______________

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-18-262
Page No. 2

FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER

Commissioner Carrasco has been a salaried employee of the Gas Company since 2005. He
has received, and will receive, an income of more than $500 during the twelve months prior to the
GWP Commission's consideration of the Project. He also owns stock in the company amounting to
over $2,000 and receives year-end incentive bonuses. In follow-up telephone conversations, you
clarified that the year-end incentive bonuses are based on a combination of company and individual
performance and could amount to as much as 25% of an employee's salary. You further clarified
that Commissioner Carrasco makes over $100,000 in his current position as Director of Regional
Public Affairs, was appointed to his position on the Commission by a City Councilmember, and he
is not compensated for the position.

The Gas Company is a regulated subsidiary of Sempra Energy, a Fortune 500 energy
services holding company. The Gas Company's reported revenue for 2017 was $11.2 billion. The
Gas Company is a publicly traded company. Currently, the Grayson Power Plant purchases its
natural gas through sources other than the Gas Company. However, it utilizes the Gas Company's
services to transport the purchased natural gas. In Fiscal Year ("FY") 2017-2018, the City paid the
Gas Company $238,232 for transportation services, which makes up less than .003% of the Gas
Company's $11.2 billion revenue.

Currently, the City, through the GWP, is evaluating proposals received in response to its
"Clean Energy Request for Proposals" ("RFP"), in order to make a recommendation to the City
Council relating to the Grayson Power Plant's "Repowering Project" ("Project"). The GWP
Commission serves as an advisory board and cannot approve projects or enter into contracts. It can,
however, make recommendations to the City Council.

If the Project is approved by the City Council, it will increase the City's purchase of natural
gas to fuel the Grayson Power Plant. The increase in the purchase of natural gas is projected to
amount to $7 million. You have stated, that under no circumstance would the Project involve the
City purchasing gas directly from the Gas Company, but the City would continue to use the Gas
Company for the transportation of the increased amount of natural gas. Because the fees paid to the
Gas Company for the transportation are based on the volume of natural gas transported, the increase
in the use of natural gas would cause an increase in the City's payment of transportation fees to the
Gas Company.

Per our telephone conversations, it is your belief that the Gas Company would continue to
be paid by the City at the same rate it is currently paid for its transportation services. Additionally,
you clarified that the Gas Company has also issued a "will serve" letter for the Project, and could
receive over $20 million in one-time equipment and installation fees, depending on the City
Council's ultimate decisions for the Project.

ANALYSIS

Section 1090 provides, in pertinent part, that "[m]embers of the Legislature, state, county,
district, judicial district, and city officers or employees shall not be financially interested in any
contract made by them in their official capacity, or by any body or board of which they are
members." Under Section 1090, "the prohibited act is the making of a contract in which the official

File No. A-18-262
Page No. 3

has a financial interest." (People v. Honig (1996) 48 Cal.App.4th 289, 333.) A contract that violates
Section 1090 is void, and the prohibition applies regardless of whether the terms of the contract are
fair and equitable to all parties. (See Becnel Advice Letter, No. A-16-097; see also Thomson v. Call
(1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.)

Section 1090 applies to all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member bodies,
whether elected or appointed, at both state and local levels. Moreover, Section 1090 also applies to
individuals in advisory positions to contracting agencies because those individuals can influence the
development of a contract during these early stages of the contracting process even though they
have no actual power to execute the final contract. (See, e.g., Schaefer v. Berinstein (1956) 140
Cal.App.2d 278, 291; City Council v. McKinley (1978) 80 Cal.App.3d 204 [member of Park and
Recreation Board who owned a landscape architectural firm participated in the making of a contract
in violation of Section 1090 where he was also a member of a committee created to advise the
Board on the design, architecture, landscaping and technical planning of a Japanese garden].) As a
member of the GWP Commission, Commissioner Carrasco is subject to Section 1090.

For purposes of this letter, the determinative issue is whether Commissioner Carrasco has a
financial interest in a contract with another company if the Gas Company will receive compensation
for transporting the additional natural gas purchased under the contract.

Although Section 1090 does not specifically define the term "financial interest," case law
and Attorney General opinions indicate that the term is to be liberally interpreted. The prohibited
financial interests may be indirect as well as direct, and may involve financial losses, or the
possibility of losses, as well as the prospect of pecuniary gain. "The defining characteristic of a
prohibited financial interest is whether it has the potential to divide an official's loyalties and
compromise the undivided representation of the public interests the official is charged with
protecting." (Lexin v. Superior Court (2010) 47 Cal.4th 1050, 1075.) "Thus, that the interest 'might
be small or indirect is immaterial so long as it is such as deprives the [people] of his overriding
fidelity to [them] and places him in the compromising situation where, in the exercise of his official
judgment or discretion, he may be influenced by personal considerations rather than the public
good.'" (Ibid.)

Here, you have noted that the increase in the use of natural gas as a result of the Project will
cause an increase in the City's payment of fees to the Gas Company. The increased money the Gas
Company would receive for transportation services of additional gas under the Project would
presumably not be for a single year, but for multiple years. You also stated that the Gas Company
could make more than $20 million on non-recurring equipment and installation fees, depending on
the City Council's ultimate decision(s) for the Project. You further clarified that the year-end
incentive houses that Commissioner Carrasco receives from the Gas Company are based on
company and individual performance, and can constitute as much as 25% of an employee's salary.
Commissioner Carrasco is the Director of Regional Public Affairs for the Gas Company and makes
over $100,000 per year.

The above circumstances establish a financial interest in the Project contracts, as they could
result in a financial benefit to the Gas Company and Commissioner Carrasco himself. Accordingly,
Section 1090 prohibits Commissioner Carrasco from participating in making any contracts related
to the Project that will implicate his financial interest in the Gas Company.

File No. A-18-262
Page No. 4

It is important to note that Section 1090 reaches beyond the officials who actually execute the contract and extends to officials who participate in the making of the contract. Participation in the making of a contract is defined broadly as any act involving preliminary discussions, negotiations, compromises, reasoning, planning, drawing of plans and specifications, and solicitation for bids. (Millbrae Assn. for Residential Survival v. City of Millbrae (1968) 262 Cal.App.2d 222, 237.) Accordingly, Section 1090 prohibits any participation in the contract by Commissioner Carrasco including, but not limited to, participating in the reports brought before the GWP Commission relating to the Project and voting on the recommendations to be made to the City Council.

We also note that although Commissioner Carrasco is prohibited from participating in the making of a contract related to the Project under Section 1090, the GWP Commission is not prohibited from doing so, including making recommendations to the City Council, so long as Commissioner Carrasco recuses himself. "[A]n advisory committee may perform its responsibilities so long as the interested member abstains and does not participate in the giving of advice." (82 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. 126, 130 (1999), citing Cal. Atty. Gen., Indexed Letter No. IL 72-143 (Aug. 16, 1992); see also Ueda Advice letter, No. A-16-104.) "They are treated differently from boards responsible for executing the actual contracts under the governing statutes and policy reasons for the prohibition. Abstention by the interested member is allowed for the one, but not for the other." (82 Ops. Cal. Atty. Gen. at p. 130.)

Because Section 1090 prohibits Commissioner Carrasco's participation in the making of a contract regarding the Project, we do not further analyze disqualification under the Act.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Dave Bainbridge
General Counsel

By: Kevin Cornwall
Counsel, Legal Division

KMC:jgl

(1985) 38 Cal.3d 633, 646.) 
. 
Section 1090 applies to all state and local officers, employees, and multi-member bodies, 
whether elected or appointed, at both state and local levels. Moreover, Section 1090 also applies to

Company employee, from taking part in the report and recommendation process relating to the city-

CONCLUSION: Yes. In general, Section 1090 prohibits Commissioner Carrasco from participating in 
the making of contracts for the Project, because such contracts will implicate his financial 
interest in the Gas Company. Prohibited conduct includes taking part in the reports 
presented to the GWP Commission, as well as voting on recommendations to be made to the 
City Council.
